messages.content: As an AI language model, I don't have personal opinions or feelings since I am not a conscious entity. My purpose is to provide useful and informative responses to your queries. Please feel free to let me know if you have any question or concern.

make "And there are still some differences in the bubble condensation rate under different subcooling degrees, which basically conforms to the law that the larger the subcooling degree is, the faster the bubble condensation is." more academic

Based on empirical observations, it can be inferred that discrepancies still persist in the rate of bubble condensation, even under varying degrees of subcooling. This trend adheres to the general principle that as the subcooling degree increases, so does the pace at which bubble condensation occurs.

As shown in Fig 22, the diameters change of several bubble under different Tsub have been obtained. And there are still some differences in the bubble condensation rate under different subcooling degrees, which basically conforms to the law that the larger the subcooling degree is, the faster the bubble condensation is.

The data illustrated in Figure 22 illustrate that alterations in the diameters of bubbles have been observed under varying degrees of subcooling. Notably, these differences in bubble condensation rates are generally consistent with the established principle that increased subcooling degree is correlated with a heightened velocity of bubble condensation.

The data illustrated in Figure 22 illustrate that alterations in the diameters of bubbles have been observed under varying degrees of subcooling. Notably, these differences in bubble condensation rates are generally consistent with the established principle that increased subcooling degree is correlated with a heightened velocity of bubble condensation.

The data presented in Figure 22 clearly demonstrate that the size of bubbles is impacted by the degree of subcooling, indicating that subcooling has an influence on bubble condensation rates. It is notable that these differences in bubble condensation rates align with the existing understanding that an elevated subcooling degree corresponds to an accelerated rate of bubble condensation.

After acquiring the bubble diameter change, basing on the method in 2.2.2, a further calculation of the paraments have been implied, and the Levenberg-Marquardt method is implied to fit the Nu heat transfer correlation, as shown in formula 2.6

Following the acquisition of the data on changes in bubble diameters, computation of additional parameters was performed using the methodology outlined in Section 2.2.2. Subsequently, the Levenberg-Marquardt algorithm was employed to fit the Nu heat transfer correlation, as depicted in Formula 2.6.

After acquiring the bubble diameter change, basing on the method in 2.2.2, a further calculation of the paraments have been implied, and the Levenberg-Marquardt method is implied to fit the Nu heat transfer correlation, as shown in formula 2.6

Upon obtaining the necessary data regarding changes in bubble diameters, additional parameters were calculated using the methodology outlined in Section 2.2.2. The Nu heat transfer correlation was then fitted using the Levenberg-Marquardt method, with the resulting equation being displayed in Formula 2.6.

Figure 23 is the three-dimensional fitting surface when the fixed Pr number is 1.981, the z-axis is the Nu number, the blue surface is the fitting surface, and the black points are the values used for fitting. It can be seen that most of the points fall near the fitting plane , a small number of points are above and below the fitting surface, the R2 of the fitting formula is 0.88, and the fitting result is relatively reliable.

Figure 23 exhibits the three-dimensional fitting surface when the Pr number is fixed at 1.981. It is evident that the majority of the points are in close proximity to the fitting plane, with only a few points being above or below the fitting surface. The R2 value of the fitting formula is 0.88, indicating a relatively reliable fitting outcome.

Figure 24 is the comparison of the Nu number obtained by fitting and the Nu number obtained by the test calculation. It can be seen that the error of the comparison value falls between -31% and 35%. It can be considered that this correlation can better represent the bubbles in liquid oxygen surface heat transfer coefficient

Figure 24 depicts a comparison between the Nu number attained through fitting and the Nu number resulting from test calculations. The comparison reveals that the error in the comparison values ranges between -31% and 35%. Based on this data, it can be concluded that this correlation effectively represents heat transfer coefficients on the surface of bubbles in liquid oxygen.
